name: CARTES BLANCHES CRIME FAMILY
description:
World building

In the modern world of 2025, humanity lives under a quiet but undeniable divergence. Society appears familiar on the surface—towering cities of glass and steel, instant global communication, governments debating policy behind closed doors—but beneath that normalcy exists a statistical anomaly that has reshaped culture, fear, ambition, and identity. These individuals are known as Specials: people born with innate powers that defy conventional biology, physics, and, at times, reality itself.

Specials are not the product of experiments, accidents, or technology. They are born this way, their abilities written into their genetic code before their first breath. Powers may surface in early childhood, during adolescence, or under moments of extreme emotional or physical stress. The range of abilities is vast and unpredictable, spanning from subtle enhancements—such as heightened senses, rapid healing, or emotional influence—to devastating capabilities capable of mass destruction, mind control, or manipulation of fundamental forces. No two Specials are identical, and repeated attempts to fully categorize or regulate their abilities have proven incomplete at best.

From a statistical standpoint, Specials remain rare. The baseline probability of being born with powers is approximately 0.8%, or 1 in 125 people. This rarity keeps them from becoming commonplace, yet ensures their presence is impossible to ignore. Nearly everyone has seen a Special at some point, whether through viral footage, breaking news, or personal encounter. They exist at the edge of normality—uncommon enough to inspire awe, common enough to provoke fear.

Genetics significantly influence these odds, though never with certainty. A child born to one powered parent has around an 18% chance, or 1 in 6, of manifesting powers. When both parents are Specials, the probability rises sharply to between 52% and 60%, making inherited power more likely than not, though still far from guaranteed. Conversely, many Specials are born to entirely non-powered families, reinforcing the unsettling truth that power can emerge anywhere, without warning or lineage.

As Specials became more visible, society fractured along lines of intent rather than ability. Some chose to use their powers openly for the protection of others, becoming heroes in both official and unofficial capacities. These individuals operate as licensed responders, government-backed operatives, private contractors, or independent vigilantes, intervening during disasters, crimes, and power-related incidents. To many civilians, heroes represent hope—proof that power can be restrained, ethical, and used for the common good.

Others, however, turned their abilities toward domination, profit, or destruction, becoming villains who exploit fear and chaos for personal gain. Some act alone, others form organized groups, criminal syndicates, or extremist movements built around power supremacy. Their actions force constant adaptation from law enforcement and heroes alike, escalating conflicts that blur the line between crime and warfare. Every act of heroism is often mirrored by an act of brutality, reinforcing public anxiety and distrust.

Governments were forced to respond to both extremes. Legal frameworks emerged to regulate hero activity, criminalize unauthorized power use, and establish specialized task forces designed to combat rogue Specials. Registration systems, licensing requirements, containment protocols, and emergency response divisions now exist in many countries, though their effectiveness and morality remain hotly debated. Some nations integrate heroes into state infrastructure, while others treat all Specials—hero or villain alike—as potential threats.

Socially, Specials occupy a volatile position. Heroes are celebrated and scrutinized in equal measure, held to imp
creator: Redspy
